Cell Manufacturing:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing: Ola's NMC→LFP pivot was validated by the 2.33x cobalt price spike ($24,000 → $56,000/MT) from DRC supply constraints. LFP 46100 cells at $80/kWh achieve cost parity with Chinese imports but still face a critical problem: the 6 GWh Gigafactory operates at ~16% utilization because Ola's scooter sales have collapsed 64% from peak. Without B2B orders (none confirmed as of May 2026), the ~4,000 Cr capex risks becoming a stranded asset.

Cell Manufacturing Opportunity

Potential Impact: 🔴 HIGH
Timeline: Medium-term (2-4 years)

---

The Opportunity

India currently imports almost all Li-ion cells. The PLI ACC Battery Scheme aims to build 50 GWh of domestic cell manufacturing capacity. Ola Electric has been awarded 20 GWh — the largest allocation to any single recipient.

---

Strategic Significance

1. Cost Reduction

  • Battery = 35% of BOM for Ola S1 Pro
  • In-house manufacturing eliminates supplier margins, import duties, logistics costs
  • Potential to reduce vehicle cost by 15-25%

2. Supply Security

  • Currently no long-term cell contracts >1 year
  • Global lithium/cobalt supply concentrated in few countries
  • Domestic manufacturing reduces geopolitical and supply disruption risk

3. New Revenue Stream

  • Supply cells to other EV OEMs
  • Energy storage systems (grid-scale, commercial, residential)
  • Export potential for cells

4. PLI Cash Incentives

  • Quarterly cash incentives based on value addition % and actual cell sales
  • 5-year incentive period from Gigafactory commissioning
  • 20 GWh total committed capacity

---

Capacity Ramp-up Plan

| Year | Capacity Target | Penalty for Missing |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| Year 1 | 1 GWh | 2x shortfall deduction from subsidy |
| Year 2 | 5 GWh | 2x shortfall deduction |
| Year 3 | 10 GWh | 2x shortfall deduction |
| Year 4 | 20 GWh | 2x shortfall deduction |

---

Prerequisites for Success

  • 4680 cell technology must pass testing and achieve commercial viability
  • Gigafactory construction on schedule
  • Raw material supply agreements for lithium, nickel, cobalt secured
  • Manufacturing yield improvement (low initial yield is expected)

---

D2C Network Moat:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing: Ola's D2C network is a double-edged sword. The 935 experience centres are company-owned (capturing 8-12% dealer margins) and provide full customer data visibility, but only ~300 had repair capability during the crisis (SERVICE_NETWORK_COMPARISON). Turnaround of 7-15 days vs TVS/Bajaj 1-2 days destroyed trust. The D2C model that was supposed to be a moat became the crisis vector — because Ola owned the entire service experience, every failure was 100% Ola's fault with no dealer buffer to absorb blame.

D2C Network Competitive Moat

Potential Impact: 🟡 MEDIUM
Timeline: Immediate

---

The Moat Thesis

Ola's 935 experience centres represent India's largest company-owned EV network. Combined with digital-first sales, this creates:
1. Direct customer data — No intermediary filtering insights
2. Uniform experience — Controlled brand presentation
3. Lower distribution cost — No dealer margins (8-12%)
4. Rapid iteration — Direct feedback loop enables faster product/service improvements

---

Network Effect Potential

As the network grows:


  • Brand visibility increases with every new centre

  • Service convenience improves with density

  • Customer acquisition cost decreases (word of mouth + visibility)

  • Data network effects — more customers = better insights = better products = more customers

---

Limitations

1. Capital intensive — Each centre requires investment; incumbents' dealer model is asset-light
2. Scale ceiling — 935 centres vs 4,000-7,000+ dealer touchpoints for TVS Motor/Bajaj Auto
3. Trust gap — Owned centres don't automatically equal trusted service (Risks/Customer Experience Gap)
4. Geographic concentration — Likely urban-heavy; rural India remains underpenetrated

---

Motorcycle Product Line:
INSIGHT: Four Motorcycle Models Announced, Roadster Entering Production in FY26
Ola has announced four electric motorcycle models -- Diamondhead, Adventure, Roadster, and Cruiser -- representing the company's expansion beyond scooters into the mass-market 2W segment. The Roadster entered production at 2% of product mix in Q4 FY26, marking Ola's first motorcycle deliveries. However, the export market requires a heavy-duty motorcycle with swappable battery for commercial use (Africa's primary use case), a product gap that the current four-model lineup does not fill.

Motorcycle Product Line

Ola Electric has announced four electric motorcycle models that extend the company's addressable market beyond scooters: the Diamondhead (performance-oriented), Adventure (touring/adventure), Roadster (commuter), and Cruiser (lifestyle/cruiser). The Roadster entered commercial production in FY26, accounting for 2% of Ola's product mix by Q4 FY26, and represents the company's first foray beyond the S1 scooter platform.

The motorcycle line addresses two strategic imperatives. First, it expands Ola's total addressable market into the motorcycle segment, which accounts for approximately 70% of India's domestic 2W market (vs ~30% for scooters). Second, it creates a product portfolio capable of addressing export markets where motorcycles (not scooters) dominate -- particularly Africa, where commercial delivery use cases demand heavy-duty motorcycle platforms.

However, a critical product gap persists. Export market analysis identifies Nigeria (200K units, 40% CAGR) and other African markets as requiring a heavy-duty motorcycle with swappable battery architecture for commercial delivery use. None of the four announced models explicitly targets this commercial-grade segment, and none incorporates the swappable battery architecture that African markets demand for Battery-as-a-Service models. Competitors including Spiro, MAX, and Ampersand already offer such products in African markets.

Key Data Points

  • Four announced models: Diamondhead, Adventure, Roadster, Cruiser
  • Roadster entered production in FY26 at 2% of Ola's quarterly product mix
  • Ola's 5-model scooter lineup (S1 range) covers Rs 69,999 to Rs 199,000 -- the widest price band in the industry
  • Motorcycles represent ~70% of India's domestic 2W market vs ~30% for scooters
  • Nigeria (Africa's largest addressable market) requires heavy-duty motorcycle with swappable battery
  • Competitors Spiro, MAX, and Ampersand already offer commercial motorcycles in Africa
  • Ola's Ola Fleet Technologies entity previously operated the experience centre network that would sell these motorcycles

Platform Evolution:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing: The Gen 2 platform's 68.85% component commonality was the unsung hero of the margin paradox. It allowed Ola to "strip down" a premium scooter into a budget variant at minimal incremental engineering cost. The S1 X at 75% mix with $100/kWh cells generates Rs 30,527/unit gross profit vs the S1 Pro at 35% mix with $130/kWh cells generating only Rs 24,300/unit. Each platform generation amplifies this effect: Gen 3 targets 80%+ commonality across scooters AND motorcycles.

Platform Evolution (Gen 3+)

Potential Impact: 🟡 MEDIUM
Timeline: Medium-term

---

The Thesis

If Gen 2 delivered 68.85% component commonality and margin improvement from -5.4% → 13.2%, future platform generations (Gen 3, Gen 4) can continue driving cost reduction and performance improvement.

---

What Gen 3+ Could Enable

1. Higher component commonality — Target 80%+ across all models (scooters + motorcycles)
2. Simplified manufacturing — Fewer unique parts = faster assembly, lower defect rates
3. Next-gen battery integration — Structural battery packs, cell-to-pack technology
4. Software-defined vehicle architecture — Centralized compute, OTA-upgradable hardware
5. Shared motorcycle + scooter platform — One platform for all 2W form factors

---

Prerequisites

1. Sustained R&D investment — ₹1,600 Cr IPO allocation committed
2. Gen 2 proven at scale — Must demonstrate reliability before next evolution
3. Gigafactory online — In-house cells enable platform-level battery optimization
4. Financial runway — Platform development takes years and significant capital

S1 X:
INSIGHT: The Margin Paradox Product
The S1 X is Ola's mass-market lifeline and its strategic trap in one. At Rs 69,999 entry price with a simpler build, it grew from 15% to 75% of sales mix (Q1 FY25 to Q3 FY26) while destroying blended ASP from Rs 1,35,000 to Rs 89,000. Yet gross margins improved from 12% to 36% in the same period — because the S1 X has fewer parts, a lower BOM, and Gigafactory cells. It saved Ola from revenue-free collapse but trapped it in a low-ASP equilibrium where every additional unit sold further depresses revenue quality.

S1 X: The Margin Paradox Product

Potential Impact: 🔴 HIGH
Timeline: Immediate (defines current strategy)

The S1 X is the product that defines Ola Electric's current competitive reality. Launched as an entry-level variant at Rs 69,999 (S1 X 2kWh) and Rs 89,999 (S1 X 3kWh), it rapidly became Ola's dominant product, growing from 15% of sales mix in Q1 FY25 to 75% by Q3 FY26. This shift was not a strategic choice but a defensive reaction: service crisis complaints drove premium S1 Pro buyers away, leaving Ola with budget-conscious customers who prioritized low price over brand trust.

The result is Ola's "margin paradox" — the S1 X has driven blended ASP from Rs 1,35,000 to Rs 89,000 (-34%), yet gross margins improved from 12% to 36% in the same period. The paradox is resolved by understanding that the S1 X has a fundamentally simpler build (fewer features, smaller battery, lower power motor) with higher component commonality (68.85% shared parts across models), and benefits from the Gigafactory's in-house cell production that reduced battery cost from $130/kWh to $85/kWh. Each S1 X unit costs less to produce relative to its selling price than premium variants.

However, the S1 X strategy creates a structural problem: Ola is now competing at price parity with Bajaj Chetak (S1 X 3kWh at Rs 89,999 vs Chetak Base at Rs 91,000), where Bajaj has 40+ years of trust inheritance, metal body construction, and the lowest service cost in the industry at Rs 400/visit. Ola's only differentiator at this price point is range and speed — but poor service reputation and limited warranty (3yr vs Hero Vida's 5yr) erode even that advantage. The Gen 3 S1 (Rs 89,000 with 8-year battery warranty and 110kg weight) attempts to address this, but its "unproven reliability" weakness means customers remain skeptical.

  • Gross margin improvement: 12% (Q4 FY25) to 36% (Q4 FY26)
  • Entry price: Rs 69,999 (S1 X 2kWh) — cheapest EV scooter in India
  • Battery cost reduction: $130/kWh (imported) to $85/kWh (in-house)
  • Platform commonality: 68.85% shared parts across S1 family
  • S1 X 3kWh vs Bajaj Chetak Base: Rs 89,999 vs Rs 91,000
  • Gen 3 platform (successor): 110kg weight, 150km range, 8-year battery warranty at Rs 89,000
  • Warranty disadvantage: 3yr vehicle + 3yr battery vs Hero's 5yr+5yr

Roadster:
INSIGHT: Roadster Is Ola's First Beachhead Into the 65% of the 2W Market It Currently Misses
Ola's Roadster motorcycle — one of four announced models (Diamondhead, Adventure, Roadster, Cruiser) — entered production in FY26 at 2% of Ola's quarterly product mix. For a company that has lost 66% of its scooter market share, the Roadster represents both a strategic imperative (the motorcycle segment is ~65% of India's 17M-unit annual 2W market) and a critical test of Ola's ability to execute product development under severe financial constraints. The Roadster is as much about survival narrative as it is about market expansion: it signals to investors that Ola has a growth story beyond scooters, even as scooter sales remain at a fraction of their peak.

Roadster

The Roadster is Ola Electric's first electric motorcycle, entering commercial production in FY26 at 2% of the company's product mix. It is one of four announced motorcycle models (with Diamondhead, Adventure, and Cruiser) that collectively represent Ola's expansion beyond scooters into the motorcycle segment — approximately 65% of India's 17 million-unit annual 2W market. For a company that has seen its scooter market share collapse from 47% to 15.9%, the motorcycle line is the most visible component of Ola's growth recovery narrative.

The Roadster's timing is consequential. The motorcycle project was announced in August 2023, when Ola commanded 35%+ scooter market share and its IPO was approaching. By the time the Roadster entered production in FY26, Ola had suffered a 66% market share loss, its stock had declined 76% from peak, and its management focus had pivoted from "Scale and Market Dominance" to "Structural Profitability and B2B Battery Supply." The Roadster is a product conceived in an era of ambition and delivered in an era of survival — a tension that defines its strategic significance.

The Roadster enters a nascent EV motorcycle market where no player has established dominance. Legacy OEMs (Hero, Bajaj, TVS) dominate ICE motorcycles but have not yet launched compelling EV alternatives. This creates a first-mover window — but Ola's execution capacity is severely constrained by its financial position (Rs 3,516 Cr cash, 12-18 month runway) and the trust deficit that crippled its scooter business. If the Roadster suffers the kind of quality issues that plagued the S1 launch, the consequences would be existential — a second service crisis in a new product category, with no brand equity buffer to absorb it.

The export connection amplifies the Roadster's importance. Africa's 380,000-unit motorcycle market (Nigeria 200K, Kenya 150K, Rwanda 30K) is primarily commercial delivery with very high Battery-as-a-Service demand — precisely the segment where a rugged electric motorcycle could compete. However, Ola's current four-model lineup does not include the heavy-duty swappable-battery motorcycle that African markets require. The Roadster is a necessary first step, but not sufficient for export markets until Ola develops BaaS capability and a commercial-grade variant.

Key Data Points

  • Roadster entered production: Q4 FY26 at 2% of product mix
  • Four announced motorcycle models: Diamondhead (performance), Adventure (touring), Roadster (commuter), Cruiser (lifestyle)
  • India motorcycle market: ~65% of 17M annual 2W units — TAM Ola has zero presence in
  • R&D spend (FY23): Rs 5,077 Mn (18.25% of income) — much allocated to motorcycle development
  • Platform commonality (scooters): 68.85% component sharing across models — replicable for motorcycles
  • Battery cost advantage: $85/kWh in-house vs $130/kWh imported — 35% reduction applicable to Roadster
  • LFP 46100 cell target: $80/kWh (Apr-2026) — ideal for motorcycle weight and cost targets
  • Export connection: 380K unit African motorcycle market (Nigeria 200K, Kenya 150K, Rwanda 30K) with BaaS demand
  • Cash runway: 12-18 months — constrains ability to scale motorcycle production
  • Development timeline: 3 years from announcement (Aug-2023) to production (FY26)

Ola Shakti:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola Shakti (residential B2B battery storage) and B2B cell supply talks represent Ola's strategic hedge against its own scooter sales collapse. With a 6 GWh Gigafactory operating at ~16% utilization (enough cells for ~1.5M scooters/year but selling only 164K), Ola must find external customers for its cells or face a massive stranded asset. However, zero confirmed B2B orders exist as of May 2026, and the global cell market is flooded with 900 GWh of Chinese overcapacity at the same $80/kWh price Ola targets.

Ola Shakti / Energy Business Pivot

Ola Electric announced its residential and commercial energy storage brand, Ola Shakti, as a strategic diversification from pure scooter manufacturing into the broader energy business. The pivot reflects a fundamental reality: the Gigafactory's 6 GWh capacity was sized for an optimistic demand scenario where Ola sold 1M+ scooters annually, but actual FY26 sales of 164,000 units consume barely 0.5 GWh of capacity. The remaining 5.5 GWh must find external buyers or become a stranded asset bleeding depreciation (Rs 566 Cr/year) and interest costs (Rs 366 Cr/year).

The B2B pivot has two channels. First, Ola Shakti targets stationary energy storage for residential, commercial, and industrial applications, leveraging LFP chemistry's thermal stability and long cycle life (3,000+ cycles vs 1,500 for NMC). India's growing energy storage market, driven by renewable integration targets and unreliable grid infrastructure, offers a natural adjacency. Second, Ola is exploring cell supply to other Indian OEMs in the E2W, E3W, and light EV segments, pitching domestic supply chain security against reliance on Chinese imports. Management's stated Q4 FY26 focus was explicitly "B2B Battery Supply & Margin Defense."

However, the B2B strategy faces a formidable headwind. The global cell manufacturing capacity reached 4,200 GWh in 2026 with ~900 GWh of excess supply, driving LFP pack prices to $80/kWh — exactly the cost Ola targets for its LFP 46100 cells. As the battery supply chain data explicitly notes: the global overcapacity is "good for Ola if buying; bad if selling B2B." Chinese manufacturers (CATL, BYD) have decades of scale advantage, established global distribution, and the ability to sustain pricing at or below cost for market share. Ola's $80/kWh cost offers zero price advantage, meaning it must compete on delivery reliability, domestic content preference, and relationship — all unproven for a company that has never sold a cell externally.

Key Data Points

  • Gigafactory capacity: 6.0 GWh (Apr-2026), targeting 12 GWh (Jul-2027), 20 GWh (Mar-2028)
  • Internal consumption: ~0.4-0.6 GWh/year at current 164K unit run-rate
  • Estimated utilization: ~16% — 84% of capacity idle
  • Management focus shifted to "B2B Battery Supply & Margin Defense" in Q4 FY26
  • Global LFP pack price: $80/kWh (down 43% from $140/kWh peak in 2022)
  • Ola's LFP 46100 cost: $80/kWh at Apr-2026 — at parity, no advantage
  • Global cell overcapacity: ~900 GWh (21% of total capacity)
  • Zero confirmed B2B orders or revenue as of May 2026
  • Ola's Revenue Per Unit rose from Rs 151,818 (FY24) to Rs 223,902 (FY26) despite ASP decline — suggesting nascent non-vehicle revenue, though no B2B revenue line exists in financial statements
  • Ola qualifies for both PLI schemes (Auto PLI + ACC Cell PLI), a dual benefit unique among Indian EV players

Export Markets:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing: The export opportunity is a strategic imperative, not a growth option. Ola's Gigafactory at 6 GWh operates at ~16% utilization. Exports can absorb ~40% of the utilization gap, but even at 100% capture of ALL viable export markets (Africa 380K + Indonesia 300K + Philippines 100K + Brazil 400K = 1.88M units), only ~65% of current 6 GWh capacity is filled. Phase 2 (12 GWh) and Phase 3 (20 GWh) would need 5-6x current African market size to justify. Export is a partial fix, not a complete solution.

Export Markets Opportunity

Potential Impact: 🟡 MEDIUM
Timeline: Long-term

---

Target Markets

Per DRHP, Ola Electric has identified:


  • Africa — Growing 2W markets, tropical climate suited for Indian vehicles

  • LATAM (Latin America) — Emerging EV adoption

  • SE Asia — Large 2W markets, similar conditions to India

---

Why Exports Matter

1. Diversification — Reduces dependence on Indian policy/competitive dynamics
2. India as manufacturing base — "China + 1" strategy benefits Indian OEMs
3. Tropical-climate advantage — Indian vehicles designed for heat, dust, rough roads
4. Value positioning — Indian OEMs compete on value-for-money globally

---

India's Export Context

  • 18% of domestic vehicle production (~4.8M vehicles) exported in FY2023
  • Global manufacturers seeking alternatives to China as production base
  • EPCG scheme: import capital goods at zero customs duty for export production

---

Prerequisites for Ola

1. Product quality must improve — Export markets have higher expectations
2. Service infrastructure — Needs local partners or D2C expansion in new geographies
3. Homologation — Each market has different regulatory requirements
4. Brand building — Zero brand recognition outside India
5. Financial stability — Export push requires investment; Ola is cash-constrained

---

Competitive Context

Bajaj Auto is already India's largest 2W exporter with established channels in Africa, LATAM, and SE Asia. They can add EVs to existing export relationships — a significant head start.

Mass Market E2W:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing: The S1 X "cannibalization" is actually the company's lifeline. At 75% of sales mix, the S1 X delivers Rs 30,527 gross profit per unit — 26% more than the S1 Pro did at 18% margin. The key insight: S1 X is NOT a cheap low-margin product. It's a cheap HIGH-margin product when powered by in-house Gigafactory cells at $85/kWh. The breakeven has dropped from ~40K to ~15K units/month.

Mass Market E2W Opportunity

Potential Impact: 🔴 HIGH
Timeline: Immediate

---

The Opportunity

Price-sensitive buyers represent the majority of India's 2W market. The Ola S1 X series (X+, X 2kWh, X 3kWh) is designed to capture this segment. Combined with B2B (fleet) and last-mile delivery, this addresses the largest addressable portion of the 2W market.

---

Product Positioning

| Model | Target Segment | Battery | Timeline |
|-------|---------------|---------|----------|
| S1 X+ | Premium mass | 3 kWh | Delivered Dec 2023 |
| S1 X (3 kWh) | Mass personal | 3 kWh | H1 FY2025 |
| S1 X (2 kWh) | Budget mass + B2B | 2 kWh | H1 FY2025 |

---

Addressable Segments

1. Personal Commuters

  • Largest segment of Indian 2W buyers
  • Price-sensitive, upgrading from entry-level ICE scooters
  • Need: reliable, low-running-cost vehicle at affordable upfront price

2. B2B / Fleet

  • Employee transportation, rental services
  • Economics-driven: TCO is the sole decision criterion
  • 45% lower TCO is a direct profit boost

3. Last-Mile Delivery

  • Zomato, Swiggy, Amazon, Flipkart delivery fleets
  • High daily mileage (80-120 km)
  • EV fuel savings compound dramatically at high usage
  • Many companies have public ESG/net-zero commitments

---

Why Ola Can Win Here

1. Gen 2 platform — 68.85% component commonality keeps costs low
2. D2C network — 935 centres enable low-cost distribution
3. PLI incentives — 13-18% of DSV improves unit economics
4. Futurefactory scale — 1M unit capacity supports volume play

---

Risks

  • Incumbents can leverage existing dealer networks for mass market
  • Quality perception matters most to value-conscious buyers
  • Subsidy changes affect affordability the most at the low end

- Subsidy erosion — FAME-II expiry (Rs 40,000→Rs 5,000) forced price-sensitive buyers to cheapest options; S1 X at Rs 69,999 became default choice

### Effects

- S1 X cannibalization paradox — 75% mix of lower-ASP S1 X actually increases gross profit per unit (+26%) because COGS fell faster than ASP

- Incumbent response in mass market — TVS (Rs 126,686/unit) and Bajaj (Rs 138,408/unit) offer better service at lower cost; Ola's Rs 800-1,500 service cost vs Bajaj Rs 400 is a structural disadvantage

- Breakeven structural improvement — EBITDA breakeven lowered from ~40K to ~15K units/month; Ola now EBITDA-positive at 35-39K units/quarter

### Amplifiers

- PLI benefits (13-18% DSV) apply to every S1 X sold — Auto PLI improves unit economics on the highest-volume model

- Gen 3 platform (launched Mar-2025) — Further reduces S1 X manufacturing complexity; targets 80%+ component commonality

- Last-mile delivery ESG mandates — Zomato, Swiggy, Amazon fleets have net-zero commitments; 45% lower TCO is compelling for B2B fleets (80-120 km/day usage)

Electric Motorcycle Market Entry

Potential Impact: 🔴 HIGH
Timeline: Medium-term (H1 FY2026)

---

The Opportunity

Motorcycles represent ~65% of India's 2W market — significantly larger than the scooter segment Ola currently operates in. Entering motorcycles opens a much larger TAM.

---

Announced Models

| Model | Positioning |
|-------|-------------|
| Diamondhead | Premium/sport |
| Adventure | Adventure touring |
| Roadster | Urban/cruiser |
| Cruiser | Classic cruiser |

All four models announced August 2023. Planned delivery: H1 FY2026.

---

Why This Matters

Market Size

  • India 2W market: ~26M vehicles annually
  • Scooters: ~35% (~9M)
  • Motorcycles: ~65% (~17M) ← Ola currently has ZERO presence here

Competitive Landscape

  • Motorcycle segment dominated by Companies/Hero MotoCorp, Bajaj Auto, TVS Motor
  • Royal Enfield, Honda also strong
  • EV motorcycle segment is nascent — first-mover opportunity

Platform Synergy

  • Replicating platform approach from scooters
  • Common EV architecture adapted to motorcycle form factors
  • Shared components reduce development cost

---

Risks

1. Development risk — No experience building motorcycles
2. Timeline risk — H1 FY2026 could slip (scooter timelines have slipped before)
3. Market acceptance — Motorcycle buyers may be more conservative, brand-loyal

5. Competition — Incumbents will defend their core motorcycle turf aggressively
